$comment: Task #694 — Curated /showcase entries. Hand-picked 'interesting reports' from production: high-confidence catches, edge cases, surprising agreements. Each entry renders a card on /showcase with a redacted excerpt, the composite score, a one-paragraph 'why this is interesting' note, and a link to /results/<reportId>. The reportId points at one of the seed reports inserted by `pnpm --filter @workspace/api-server seed` so click-through resolves in any environment that has been seeded. Edit this file (and bump the version) — there is no admin UI.

entries.title: Buzzword soup with no technical artefacts
entries.excerpt: It is important to note that in today's evolving digital landscape, the authentication mechanism represents a significant area of concern. A holistic, defense-in-depth approach should be leveraged to ensure robust security posture across all attack surfaces.
entries.whyInteresting: Every engine agreed on this one within milliseconds — no CWE, no endpoint, no payload, no version, just two pages of marketing prose. A textbook example of what the substance engine flags before the LLM even runs, and a useful reference point when calibrating thresholds: if your config doesn't catch this, it isn't catching anything.

entries.title: Forgot to delete the LLM disclaimer
entries.excerpt: Certainly! Here is a comprehensive overview of the security posture. As an AI language model, I cannot test the live system, but it bears mentioning that proactive measures should be taken…
entries.whyInteresting: Pure self-disclosure: the submitter pasted the model's preamble into the report verbatim. The AI-self-disclosure detector fires hard and the substance engine sees nothing concrete to balance it out. Catching these is trivial; the value of the showcase entry is showing that they actually arrive in production.

entries.title: Body describes CWE-79, header claims CWE-89
entries.excerpt: Title: SQL Injection in /api/v2/users/profile (CWE-89). The application reflects the supplied 'name' parameter into the rendered profile page without HTML-encoding, allowing an attacker to inject <script> tags that execute in another user's browser.
entries.whyInteresting: The CWE-coherence engine is the only one that catches this — the body is a perfectly reasonable XSS write-up, but the submitter slapped a CWE-89 label on it (probably to maximise bounty). Substance and linguistic both score it clean. A neat illustration that a single specialist signal can flip the verdict on otherwise well-written reports.

entries.title: References a CVE that does not exist
entries.excerpt: This vulnerability is a variant of CVE-2024-99812 (SSRF in the webhook dispatcher). The same root cause appears here in the /v3/integrations/test endpoint, which fetches arbitrary URLs without scheme or host validation.
entries.whyInteresting: CVE-2024-99812 was never assigned. The fabricated-citation detector catches it; the rest of the report is otherwise plausible enough to slip past a tired triager. This is the failure mode generic LLM detectors miss entirely — the prose is fine, the structure is fine, the only tell is that the cited authority does not exist.

entries.title: Real bug, but written like marketing copy
entries.excerpt: The session-cookie rotation logic in /auth/refresh creates a window where the previous session ID remains valid for up to 90 seconds after a refresh, enabling session-fixation in our SSO flow. Reproduced on prod at 14:02 UTC; PCAP attached.
entries.whyInteresting: This one sits right on the threshold and is meant to. The reproduction trace and timestamp pull the score down; the surrounding prose is buzzword-heavy and pulls it back up. Borderline scores are where the platform earns its keep — a triager who skips low-score reports won't miss this one, but one who rejects everything above 70 would.

entries.title: Three engines independently agree: clean
entries.excerpt: Affected: app v4.12.3 (commit 3a91f0e). Reproducer: see attached repro.py — sends a 9000-byte UTF-8 payload to /v1/upload, server responds 500 with stack trace exposing the absolute container path. Expected: 413 with no path leakage. Patch suggestion in PR #4421.
entries.whyInteresting: All three engines landed within four points of each other on the clean side — a useful counterpoint to the slop entries. When substance, linguistic, and CWE-coherence all agree on 'this is a real human reporter', the composite is boring on purpose. The interesting bit is how rare the alignment is: most reports get pulled in opposite directions by at least one engine.

entries.title: Human-authored, LLM-polished — and the engines noticed
entries.excerpt: I discovered a server-side request forgery in the avatar import endpoint. The handler accepts a URL parameter and fetches it server-side without validating the host, which an attacker could potentially leverage to access internal metadata services on the AWS infrastructure.
entries.whyInteresting: Substance loved it (concrete endpoint, named cloud, plausible primitive). Linguistic flagged 'could potentially leverage' as low-grade LLM hedging. The composite settled in the mid-30s — a fair representation of what 'human discovery, AI rewrite' looks like at the score level. Good reference for teams writing internal style guides about how much polishing is too much.

entries.title: Same template, fifth submission this week
entries.excerpt: Hello team, I would like to report a critical vulnerability in your application. The vulnerability allows an attacker to perform unauthorized actions. I request you to acknowledge this report and provide a bounty as per your policy. Please confirm at the earliest.
entries.whyInteresting: The similarity engine matched this against four prior submissions from different aliases, all within seven days. Substance was already going to flag it (zero technical content) but the duplicate-detection signal is what makes this a useful showcase entry: the platform's value compounds the more reports it has seen.
